EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked southeast of the Gulf of Maine during the 30th. A narrow band of heavy snow developed across Washington and Hancock counties during the morning then remained nearly stationary through much of the afternoon. Snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour occurred within the band. The band then slowly moved east during the evening. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred during the afternoon. A sharp gradient of snow occurred in the vicinity of the band. Snow totals of 9 to 14 inches...with local totals up to 17 inches...occurred within the core of the band from Princeton in Washington county to Bar Harbor in Hancock county. Snow totals rapidly decreased to 5 to 8 inches along the edges of the core to 1 to 4 inches just west of the band.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 7 to 14 inches...with local totals up to around 17 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure over the Great Lakes on the morning of the 16th slid southeastward to off the New Jersey coast by the morning of the 17th, then out to sea.  The storm brought 5 to 8 inches of snow to coastal sections of western Maine with lesser amounts inland.

The energy from a storm slipping southeast from the Great Lakes merged with the energy from low pressure off the southeast U.S. coast to form an intense area of low pressure off the mid-Atlantic coast by the morning of January 4th.  The intense low brought heavy snow and high winds to much of the region, with blizzard conditions along the southwest coast.  In addition, the storm brought coastal flooding and erosion to the coast.  The storm brought 10 to 15 inches of snow to much of western Maine.

Low pressure tracking across the eastern Gulf of Maine brought heavy snow to portions of Downeast Maine. Snow developed during the early morning hours of the 17th then persisted into the evening. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred through the afternoon hours. Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 6 to 10 inches from coastal portions of Hancock to central Washington county...with 7 to 13 inches across coastal Washington county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 7 to 13 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very warm temperatures combined with 1 to 3 inches of rain to produce ice jams and flooding on the Kennebec River in Augusta (flood stage 12.0 ft), which crested at 19.58 ft. Several cars were damaged in Hallowell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted across the region through the overnight hours of the 11th into the 12th bringing unusually warm air. A slow moving Arctic cold front then settled across the region through the night of the 12th into the 13th while low pressure tracked along the front. Precipitation began in the form of rain in advance of the Arctic front. The rain transitioned to snow...sleet and freezing rain across northern areas through the evening of the 12th into the early morning hours of the 13th. Storm total sleet accumulations across northern Aroostook county ranged from 1 to 2 inches along with between a tenth and two tenths of an inch of freezing rain. Snow accumulations of 4 to 6 inches occurred across northwest Aroostook county...with 1 to 3 inches across northeast areas. Warning criteria sleet accumulations occurred during the morning of the 13th.|Record high temperatures and record daily rainfall occurred across portions of Downeast Maine in the warm sector of the storm. Storm total rainfall across Downeast Maine ranged from 2.00 to 3.00 inches...with local totals up to around 4.00 inches...from the 12th into the 13th. The warm temperatures and rain also contributed to rapidly melting snow. Several roads were flooded and damaged. In Washington county...Route 191 in East Machias was damaged. In Hancock county flooding and road damage was reported on Norway Drive in Bar Harbor...Molasses Pond Road in Eastbrook and Torrey Brook Road on Deer Isle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 4 to 6 inches...along with 1 to 2 inches of sleet and one to two tenths of an inch of freezing rain.

Rapidly intensifying low pressure tracked from the Mid-Atlantic region toward southwest Nova Scotia during the 4th...exiting across the Maritimes during the 5th. The low brought blizzard conditions to all of northern and eastern Maine. Snow developed across Downeast areas during the morning of the 4th...then expanded northward across the remainder of the region through the early afternoon hours. Blizzard conditions developed around noon across Downeast areas then expanded northward across the remainder of the region through the afternoon.|Wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph occurred along the Downeast coast...with gusts of 35 to 45 mph across the remainder of the region. Snowfall rates of 2 to 3 inches per hour at times occurred in the heavier snow bands during the peak of the storm. The snow mixed with sleet and freezing rain along the Downeast coast during the evening of the 4th ending blizzard conditions there. Blizzard conditions persisted through the remainder of the evening into the early morning hours of the 5th across the remainder of the region. Storm total snow accumulations across coastal Washington county ranged from 7 to 10 inches...with 10 to 14 inches inland from the coast. Snow accumulations across the remainder of the region generally ranged from 12 to 20 inches...with local totals up to around 24 inches.|The storm occurred during a time of unusually high tides. The combination of unusually high tides...strong winds and large waves contributed to coastal flooding mostly during the early afternoon high tide on the 4th. A record high water level of 15.04 feet MLLW was reported at the Bar Harbor NOAA NOS tide gage. Recent cold temperatures had allowed more extensive ice than usual to form along the coast. The high tides and large waves dislodged this ice. The floating ice helped damage ships and wharfs in harbors...along with enhancing beach erosion. Significant beach erosion was reported at Swans Island along with damage to wharfs and boats...with a dock washed away on Great Cranberry Island. A historic smokehouse was partially collapsed in Lubec...with the remnants floated around 100 yards before grounding. Coastal roads were also closed and damaged with debris washed on to the roads. Seawall Road was left nearly impassable due to rocks and debris washed on to the road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 5 to 10 inches along with blizzard conditions.

Snow developed across Downeast areas during the morning of the 4th...then expanded northward across the remainder of the region through the early afternoon hours. Blizzard conditions developed around noon across Downeast areas then expanded northward across the remainder of the region through the afternoon.|Wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph occurred along the Downeast coast...with gusts of 35 to 45 mph across the remainder of the region. Snowfall rates of 2 to 3 inches per hour at times occurred in the heavier snow bands during the peak of the storm. The snow mixed with sleet and freezing rain along the Downeast coast during the evening of the 4th ending blizzard conditions there. Blizzard conditions persisted through the remainder of the evening into the early morning hours of the 5th across the remainder of the region. Storm total snow accumulations across coastal Washington county ranged from 7 to 10 inches...with 10 to 14 inches inland from the coast. Snow accumulations across the remainder of the region generally ranged from 12 to 20 inches...with local totals up to around 24 inches.|The storm occurred during a time of unusually high tides. The combination of unusually high tides...strong winds and large waves contributed to coastal flooding mostly during the early afternoon high tide on the 4th. A record high water level of 15.04 feet MLLW was reported at the Bar Harbor NOAA NOS tide gage. Recent cold temperatures had allowed more extensive ice than usual to form along the coast. The high tides and large waves dislodged this ice. The floating ice helped damage ships and wharfs in harbors...along with enhancing beach erosion. Significant beach erosion was reported at Swans Island along with damage to wharfs and boats...with a dock washed away on Great Cranberry Island. A historic smokehouse was partially collapsed in Lubec...with the remnants floated around 100 yards before grounding. Coastal roads were also closed and damaged with debris washed on to the roads. Seawall Road was left nearly impassable due to rocks and debris washed on to the road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 10 to 14 inches along with blizzard conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front crossed the region from the night of the 22nd into the morning of the 23rd in advance of low pressure. The low then tracked west of the region during the 23rd. Snow developed through the evening of the 22nd into the morning of the 23rd. The snow then mixed with sleet and freezing rain from the late morning into the afternoon of the 23rd...with a wintry mix persisting into the early morning hours of the 24th. Warning criteria precipitation occurred during the morning of the 23rd. Snow accumulations across northern Aroostook county ranged from 10 to 15 inches...along with 1 to 2 inches of sleet and two to three tenths of an inch of freezing rain. Across northern portions of Somerset...Piscataquis and Penobscot counties snow accumulations generally ranged from 5 to 8 inches along with around 1 inch of sleet. Freezing rain also accumulated two to three tenths of an inch across northern Penobscot county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 10 to 14 inches along with 1 to 2 inches of sleet and two to three tenths of an inch of freezing rain.

Across northern portions of Somerset...Piscataquis and Penobscot counties snow accumulations generally ranged from 5 to 8 inches along with around 1 inch of sleet. Freezing rain also accumulated two to three tenths of an inch across northern Penobscot county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 5 to 8 inches along with around 1 inch of sleet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front crossed the region from the night of the 22nd into the morning of the 23rd in advance of low pressure. The low then tracked west of the region during the 23rd. Snow developed through the evening of the 22nd into the morning of the 23rd. The snow then mixed with sleet and freezing rain from the late morning into the afternoon of the 23rd...with a wintry mix persisting into the early morning hours of the 24th. Warning criteria precipitation occurred during the morning of the 23rd. Snow accumulations across northern Aroostook county ranged from 10 to 15 inches...along with 1 to 2 inches of sleet and two to three tenths of an inch of freezing rain. Across northern portions of Somerset...Piscataquis and Penobscot counties snow accumulations generally ranged from 5 to 8 inches along with around 1 inch of sleet. Freezing rain also accumulated two to three tenths of an inch across northern Penobscot county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 5 to 8 inches along with around 1 inch of sleet and two to three tenths of an inch of freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed off the Florida coast on the 3rd of January before moving north on the 4th. The intense low (956 mb) passed southeast of Nantucket Island during a period of very high astronomical tides. The forecast astronomical tide at Portland was 11.6 feet, close to its 12 foot flood stage. Storm force northeasterly winds developed at high tide , however nearshore waves only reached 8 to 12 feet during this short span of time. The winds produced a storm surge of 2.2 feet in Portland Harbor. The resulting storm tide topped out at 13.8 feet, which was the third highest in modern history (records go back to 1912). The tide remained above flood stage for nearly three hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure combined with high astronomical tides to produce coastal flooding in Lincolnville. Water was 6 to 12 inches deep on Route 1 in Lincolnville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed off the Florida coast on the 3rd of January before moving north on the 4th. In Wells, a water rescue occurred on Long Beach Avenue with two feet of water covering the road. Webhannet Drive was also flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The energy from a storm slipping southeast from the Great Lakes merged with the energy from low pressure off the southeast U.S. coast to form an intense area of low pressure off the mid-Atlantic coast by the morning of January 4th.  The intense low brought heavy snow and high winds to much of the region, with blizzard conditions along the southwest coast.  In addition, the storm brought coastal flooding and erosion to the coast.  The storm brought 10 to 15 inches of snow to much of western Maine.

A fast moving intensifying low approached the region late on the 7th...crossed the Gulf of Maine overnight...then exited across the Maritimes. Snow developed through the afternoon of the 7th...then persisted into the early morning hours of the 8th. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred through the evening of the 7th. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 6 to 10 inches...with local totals up to around 12 inches across southeast Aroostook county. The snow transitioned to a wintry mix and rain along the Downeast coast and portions of interior Downeast Maine limiting snow accumulations there.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 8 to 12 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed off the New Jersey coast (981mb), on March 2nd before rapidly intensifying well offshore on March 3rd (974mb), producing hurricane force wind gusts in the Gulf of Maine. The storm then tracked in a loop and retrograded back towards the southern New England coastline. This occurred during the highest astronomical tide of the month, leading to moderate coastal flooding from Portland through York and Rockingham counties. As the system moved back offshore and became quasi-stationary, large, long period swells reached the coast for several consecutive tide cycles from March 3rd through the 5th and caused major beach erosion, splash-over, and coastal inundation in local areas along the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On March 2nd the highest astronomical tide of the month occurred in Portland at 11.2 feet. An additional storm surge of 1.9 feet allowed the storm tide to top out at 13.1 feet, which was the eleventh highest tide ever recorded in the Forest City. The Portland Harbor tide gage was over flood stage for a full 152 minutes. Thereafter, storm damage during the period of March 3-5th was mainly driven by high energy, long period swells which topped out at 28 feet at the Western Shelf Buoy, with wave periods around 16 seconds. Some of the most significant beach erosion within the last 30 years was documented by citizen volunteers and government surveys. Beach erosion, splash-over and coastal flooding was widespread and occurred in Scarborough along Pine Point Road and at the Clambake Restaurant. In Portland, water levels reached the doors of J's Oyster Bar, while flood waters greater than two feet covered Marginal Way. Biddeford emergency management officials reported over three feet of water on Granite Point Road for one and a half miles where seven homes where damaged as well as one home at Hills Beach. Boulders used as armor at the top of beaches in Biddeford were moved by powerful waves. Mile Stretch and Fortunes Rocks Roads also suffered significant flooding and erosion damage. In Scarborough, Sawyer Street and Pine Point Road at East Grand Avenue were closed. In Wells, Mile Road, Webhannet Drive, Eldrige Road, Bourne Avenue and Ocean Avenue were closed due to flooding. Two people in the community were rescued from their stalled van in flood waters using a front-end loader. Water flooded and significantly damaged roadways a foot deep in Kennebunkport including Beach Avenue as well as flooding businesses and cars in parking lots reminiscent of the recent January, 2018 coastal flood. In York, a 200 year old shipwreck was unearthed at Short Sands Beach due to severe beach erosion. In the same town, water was up to the Seawall's Bridge deck and the elevated path leading out to the Wiggley Bridge was submerged under a foot of water.

Two people in the community were rescued from their stalled van in flood waters using a front-end loader. Water flooded and significantly damaged roadways a foot deep in Kennebunkport including Beach Avenue as well as flooding businesses and cars in parking lots reminiscent of the recent January, 2018 coastal flood. In York, a 200 year old shipwreck was unearthed at Short Sands Beach due to severe beach erosion. In the same town, water was up to the Seawall's Bridge deck and the elevated path leading out to the Wiggley Bridge was submerged under a foot of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed off the New Jersey coast (981mb), on March 2nd before rapidly intensifying well offshore on March 3rd (974mb), producing hurricane force wind gusts in the Gulf of Maine. The storm then tracked in a loop and retrograded back towards the southern New England coastline. This occurred during the highest astronomical tide of the month, leading to moderate coastal flooding from Portland through York and Rockingham counties. As the system moved back offshore and became quasi-stationary, large, long period swells reached the coast for several consecutive tide cycles from March 3rd through the 5th and caused major beach erosion, splash-over, and coastal inundation in local areas along the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On March 2nd the highest astronomical tide of the month occurred in Portland at 11.2 feet. An additional storm surge of 1.9 feet allowed the storm tide to top out at 13.1 feet, which was the eleventh highest tide ever recorded in the Forest City. The Portland Harbor tide gage was over flood stage for a full 152 minutes. Thereafter, storm damage during the period of March 3-5th was mainly driven by high energy, long period swells which topped out at 28 feet at the Western Shelf Buoy, with wave periods around 16 seconds. Some of the most significant beach erosion within the last 30 years was documented by citizen volunteers and government surveys. Beach erosion, splash-over and coastal flooding was widespread and occurred in Scarborough along Pine Point Road and at the Clambake Restaurant. In Portland, water levels reached the doors of J's Oyster Bar, while flood waters greater than two feet covered Marginal Way. Biddeford emergency management officials reported over three feet of water on Granite Point Road for one and a half miles where seven homes where damaged as well as one home at Hills Beach. In the same town, water was up to the Seawall's Bridge deck and the elevated path leading out to the Wiggley Bridge was submerged under a foot of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure off the Southeast U.S. coast on the morning of the 12th intensified rapidly as it moved slowly northeast to just southeast of Cape Cod by the morning of the 13th.  The low continued its slow movement to Nova Scotia by the morning of the 14th.  ||The storm brought heavy snow to the all of western Maine with the heaviest snow falling from interior York to southern Oxford.  Snowfall amounts ranged from about a foot in the western mountains to more than 24 inches across portions of York County.||In addition, the storm brought several hours of blizzard conditions along the coast during the late morning and early afternoon of the 13th.

Low pressure intensified off the mid Atlantic coast (993 mb) on March 7th and moved to just off the northern New England coast by March 8th (989 mb). The low produced storm-force wind gusts over the outer waters. The storm then tracked in a small loop and retrograded back towards the Maine coast. Although this event occurred during a period of low astronomical tides, tides were still running high from a large ocean storm several days earlier. The impact from this system was enhanced because much of the coastal armor such as dune systems and rocky revetments had been compromised or destroyed from the storm earlier in the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ocean storm triggered a storm surge of over two feet at the time of high tide, yet tide levels never reached flood stage. Beach erosion and splash-over were reported in Saco as 20 foot waves coincided with the time of high tide. This caused damage to area roads. Eastern Avenue in Saco was eroded near the intersection with Pearl Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving intensifying low approached the region late on the 7th...crossed the Gulf of Maine overnight...then exited across the Maritimes. Snow developed through the afternoon of the 7th...then persisted into the early morning hours of the 8th. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred through the evening of the 7th. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 6 to 10 inches...with local totals up to around 12 inches across southeast Aroostook county. The snow transitioned to a wintry mix and rain along the Downeast coast and portions of interior Downeast Maine limiting snow accumulations there.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 6 to 9 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracked toward Nova Scotia on the 13th...then lifted across New Brunswick during the 14th. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure over the Southeast U.S. on the morning of the 6th moved offshore and began to intensify during the day as it moved slowly northeast to the Virginia Capes by the morning of the 7th.  The storm spread snow across the area during the day of the 7th as it continued to move slowly northeast.  Heavy snow developed overnight with the storm reaching the Gulf of Maine by the morning of the 8th.  Snow eventually tapered off during the late afternoon and evening as the storm moved away from the region.||Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 10 to 20 inches across western Maine.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and snow melt combined to cause brief minor flooding on the Kennebec River at Skowhegan (flood stage 35k cfs), which crested at 36,669 cfs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain and melting snow produced flooding along the Mattawamkeag River. The river rose above flood stage late on April 26th...crested around 2 feet above flood stage early on the 30th...then fell below flood stage during the afternoon of May 4th. The flooding mostly impacted low lying marshy areas along the river. However...a portion of the Bancroft Road between Wytopitlock and Danforth was closed for nearly a week due to the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding along the Mattawamkeag River caused the closure of a portion of the Bancroft Road...between Wytopitlock and Danforth...for nearly a week.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure lifting across Maine drew a strong cold front across the region during the afternoon and evening of the 3rd. West to northwest winds rapidly increased in the wake of the cold front and persisted into the early morning hours of the 4th. Winds were sustained at 25 to 35 mph...with gusts of 50 to 60 mph. The winds toppled and snapped numerous trees along with breaking branches. The falling trees and branches brought down power lines contributing to power outages...along with snapped utility poles. Power outages rapidly increased through the evening of the 3rd into the early morning hours of the 4th. Power outages peaked at around 45,000 during the early morning hours of the 4th...which included additional power outages across central and Downeast portions of the region. Trees and utility poles blocked some roads slowing the recovery process. Winds then diminished later during the morning of the 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph...with gusts of 50 to 60 mph...toppled or snapped numerous trees and broke branches which brought down power lines contributing to power outages. Utility poles were also snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracked along the New England coast to the Gulf of Maine during the 13th...exiting across the Maritimes during the early morning hours of the 14th. Snow expanded north across the region through the early morning hours of the 13th. Snow...heavy at times...persisted into the afternoon then diminished during the evening. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred through the afternoon into the early evening. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 6 to 9 inches...with local totals to around 10 inches...across northern areas. A snow and rain mix across central areas and a transition to rain Downeast limited snow accumulations in those areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 6 to 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An above average seasonal snowpack and cold start to April which delayed snow melt contributed to an above average late April snowpack across the region. Several lows crossing the region brought 1.00 to 2.00 inches of rain to northern Maine from the 25th into the 27th. At the same time...warm temperatures supported a rapid snowmelt which released several inches of additional water from the snowpack. With the ground already nearly saturated...runoff rapidly entered rivers and streams leading to rising water levels. Rivers and streams began to overflow their banks during the 26th. Numerous roads were flooded to varying depths and closed across Aroostook county. Some roads were able to re-open once the flooding subsided later on the 27th into the 28th. However...the flooding did damage culverts and bridges on some roads. Numerous logging roads were also washed out or damaged to varying degrees across the North Maine Woods with this event. Monetary damages with this event are unknown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads were flooded and closed due to run-off from heavy rain and snowmelt. Some roads were able to re-open once the water subsided. However...the flooding did damage some culverts and bridges which needed repairs. Portions of some roads were washed out. Numerous logging roads in the North Maine Woods were particularly impacted from the flooding with washouts and varying degrees of damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure crossed the region during the 4th bringing heavy snow to northwest portions of Aroostook county. Snow began during the early morning hours of the 4th and persisted into the evening. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred  from late morning to around noon. Storm total snow accumulations across northwest Aroostook county generally ranged from 8 to 12 inches...with local totals up to 14 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 8 to 12 inches...with local totals up to 14 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An above average seasonal snowpack and cold start to April which delayed snow melt contributed to an above average late April snowpack. Rainfall from the last week of April through the first week of May generally ranged from 2.00 to 3.00 inches. This rain...along with melting of the remaining snowpack which released significant amounts of water...contributed to flooding of lakes and rivers across northern Aroostook county. The Fish River at Fort Kent rose above flood stage during the evening of April 29th. The river then crested around a foot above flood stage...at moderate flood stage...from May 3rd into the 4th. The river crested at the second highest level ever on record. The river then slowly fell...dropping below flood stage during the evening of the 7th. Flooding along the Fish River resulted in the closure of  roads and bridges. The flooding also impacted homes and camps (seasonal homes) along the river in the Soldier Pond area along with the Fish River chain of lakes. The Saint John River at Fort Kent crested slightly above flood stage early on the 1st. The river fell back slightly below flood stage later on the 1st. However...the river remained near flood stage for several days thereafter. Flood impacts from the Saint John River at Fort Kent were mostly confined to areas along the immediate river including the temporary closing of River Road and the flooding of parks.|Rising lake levels began to cause problems late on April 29th into the 30th with waters starting to encroach on homes and camps located along the lakeshores. Lake levels continued to rise into early May eventually flooding basements and reaching the first floor of some homes. The lakes were still largely ice covered when water levels began to rise. The rising lakes and gusty winds eventually drove ice into some homes and camps. The pressure of the ice collapsed decks...damaged structures and forced some buildings off their foundation. Lake flood damage was reported on Portage Lake...Saint Froid Lake...Eagle Lake...Square Lake...Cross Lake and Long Lake. Some of the most significant impacts occurred around Portage Lake where numerous homes and camps were damaged due to the flooding and ice. On Long Lake...the causeway leading to Pelletier Island was flooded and closed. An estimated 65 buildings...many of them seasonal homes...were impacted to varying degrees by the lake flooding and ice. This event persisted into early May when the majority of the impacts occurred. Saturated ground along with runoff from rain and melting snow also contributed to a creep landslide on the River Road in Caribou which significantly damaged a portion of the road. The included monetary damages represent damage to public infrastructure.  A significant portion of this money was related to road damage due to the landslide. Monetary damage to private property with this event is unknown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: River and lake flooding occurred from late April into early May due to run-off from rain and melting of the remaining snowpack. The most significant impacts occurred along the Fish River and lakes across extreme northeast Aroostook county. Homes and other structures were flooded and damaged to varying degrees. Lake ice also contributed to the damage...destroying decks and forcing some buildings off their  foundation. Roads were flooded and closed with damage to some roads...culverts and bridges.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front and upper level disturbance crossed the region during the afternoon of the 10th. Thunderstorms developed in advance of the cold front. Some of the thunderstorms became severe producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail ranged from nickel to quarter size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong convection developed on a pre-frontal trough ahead of an approaching cold front on the afternoon of June 18th. A deep layer of moderate to strong shear accompanied this boundary as it pushed in from the west. Moderate helicity values resulted in good rotation with some of these storms and at least one tornado was confirmed along with several reports of funnel clouds. The majority of these storms produced straight line wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees in South Gorham.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heat wave with unusually persistent hot and humid conditions occurred across the region from July 2nd through the 5th. Temperatures in the upper 80s and 90s along with dewpoints in the 70s produced heat index readings generally ranging through the 90s. During this time...heat related emergency room visits and requests for ambulance transport increased. The greatest heat related health impacts occurred with adults aged 65 and older. However...no deaths were considered directly attributable to the heat. A cold front crossing the region during the 6th broke the heat wave.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum Heat Index values generally ranged through the 90s during the heat wave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain and melting snow produced flooding along the Mattawamkeag River. The river rose above flood stage on April 28th...crested around 2 feet above flood stage early on the 30th...then fell below flood stage during the afternoon of the 4th. The flooding mostly impacted low lying marshy areas along the river. However...a portion of the Bancroft Road between Wytopitlock and Danforth was closed for nearly a week due to the flooding. This was an event which began in late April and persisted into early May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding along the Mattawamkeag River led to the closure of a portion of the Bancroft Road...between Wytopitlock and Danforth...for nearly a week.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An above average seasonal snowpack and cold start to April which delayed snow melt contributed to an above average late April snowpack. Rainfall from the last week of  April through the first week of May generally ranged from 2.00 to 3.00 inches. This rain...along with melting of the remaining snowpack which released significant amounts of water...contributed to flooding of lakes and rivers across northeastern Aroostook county. The Fish River at Fort Kent rose above flood stage during the evening of April 29th. The river then crested around a foot above flood stage...at moderate flood stage...from the 3rd into the 4th. The river crested at the second highest level ever on record. The river then slowly fell...dropping below flood stage during the evening of the 7th. Flooding along the Fish River led to the closure of roads and bridges. The flooding also impacted homes and camps (seasonal homes) along the river in the Soldier Pond area along with the Fish River chain of lakes. The Saint John River at Fort Kent crested slightly above flood stage early on the 1st. However...the river remained near flood stage fort several days thereafter. Flood impacts from the Saint John River at Fort Kent were mostly confined to areas along the immediate river including the temporary closure of River Road and the flooding of parks.|Rising lake levels began to cause problems late on April 29th into the 30th with waters starting to encroach on homes and camps located along the lakeshores. Lake levels continued to rise into early May eventually flooding basements and reaching the first floor of some homes. The lakes were still largely ice covered when water levels began to rise. The rising lakes and gusty winds eventually drove ice into some homes and camps. The pressure of the ice collapsed decks...damaged structures and forced some buildings off their foundation. Lake flood damage was reported on Portage Lake...Saint Froid Lake...Eagle Lake...Square Lake...Cross Lake and Long Lake. Some of the most significant impacts occurred  around Portage Lake where numerous homes and camps were damaged due to the flooding and ice. On Long Lake...the causeway leading to Pelletier Island was flooded and closed. An estimated 65 buildings...many of them seasonal homes...were impacted to varying degrees by the lake flooding and ice. Saturated ground along with runoff from rain and melting snow also contributed to a creep landslide on the River Road in Caribou which significantly damaged a portion of the road. This river and lake flooding was an event which began in late April and persisted into early May. Monetary damages with this event were included with the April summary. The included monetary damages represented damage to public infrastructure. A significant portion of that money was related to road damage due to the landslide. Monetary damage to private property with this event is unknown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: River and lake flooding occurred from late April into early May due to run-off from rain and melting of the remaining snowpack. The most significant impacts occurred along the Fish River and lakes across extreme northeast Aroostook county. Homes and structures were flooded and damaged to varying degrees. Lake ice also contributed to the damage...destroying decks and forcing some buildings off their foundation. Roads were flooded and closed with damage to some roads...culverts and bridges.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked across the Gulf of Maine through the 17th...exiting across the Maritimes during the early morning hours of the 18th. Snow developed across the region through the morning of the 17th then persisted into the early morning hours of the 18th. Based on the duration of the event...warning criteria snow accumulations occurred from the late evening of the 17th into the early morning hours of the 18th. Storm total snow accumulations in the warning area generally ranged from 9 to 13 inches. Across much of the remainder of eastern Maine snow totals generally ranged from 6 to 10 inches. Lesser snow accumulations occurred across western areas. A transition to a wintry mix and rain limited snow accumulations along the Downeast coast.  Winds gusting up to 30 to 40 mph in the wake of the storm produced blowing and drifting snow across northern and central portions of the region through the morning into the afternoon of the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 9 to 12 inches.

A strong squall line formed during the late morning as it moved into central and southern Maine producing numerous reports of wind damage all the way down to the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees and power lines in Naples.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracked northeast along the Saint Lawrence River Valley from late on the 21st through the morning of the 22nd. The low drew unseasonably warm air north across the region. Light freezing rain accumulated .10 to .20 of an inch across northern and central areas during the morning of the 21st before a transition to rain. Across Downeast areas...precipitation generally began in the form of rain. A strong low level jet crossed the region through the afternoon of of the 21st into the early morning hours of the 22nd drawing warm air northward. Southerly winds gusted to 30 to 40 mph across the region during this time...with some gusts up to around 50 mph along the Downeast coast.|Abundant moisture was also drawn north producing heavy rain. Rainfall totals of 1.00 to 2.00 inches occurred across much of the region...with 2.00 to 3.00 inches Downeast and up to around 3.50 inches along the Downeast coast. High temperatures from the 21st to the 22nd generally ranged from the mid 40s to around 50 north with around 50 to the lower 50s Downeast. The rain and unseasonably warm temperatures melted significant amounts of snow. Runoff from the rain and melting snow caused rising water levels on rivers and streams. The rising waters caused ice to move and jam on some central and northern rivers. Elevated water levels due to an ice jam on the Pleasant River near Milo closed Pleasant Street for several hours during the afternoon and evening of the 22nd. Other ice jams were reported though they did not cause flooding. |Onshore winds...large waves and a high astronomical tide led to coastal flooding along portions of the Hancock county coast during the morning high tide on the 22nd. Overwash and coastal flooding were reported along Seawall Road and the Deer Isle Causeway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam on the Pleasant River caused rising water levels. The waters eventually flooded a portion of Pleasant Street..near Milo...causing the road to be closed for nearly 6 hours during the afternoon and early evening. Water levels fell once the ice jam released allowing the road to re-open.

Overwash and coastal flooding were reported along Seawall Road and the Deer Isle Causeway...with small debris left on the roads. Flooding ended with the falling tide.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight-line winds from a severe thunderstorm downed numerous trees in the Boothbay area.  While scattered snapped or uprooted trees were seen throughout the Town, the most damage was observed between the Back River...and  Back River and Knickerbocker Roads.  Winds throughout much of the Boothbay area were estimated to be between 50 and 70 mph.  The greatest amount of damage was observed |in an area between Jacobs Landing and Tidewater Road where numerous |large trees were snapped or uprooted by the storm.  In this area, |winds were estimated to have gusted between 70 and 90 mph.  Six to eight power poles were snapped by trees falling on power lines along Back Road..

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very hot and humid air mass was in place over the region on the afternoon of August 7th with temperatures in the 90s and dew points in the lower 70s producing CAPE values up to 3000 J/KG. An upper trough approaching from the west brought increasing shear into the region as storms began developing in the afternoon. Multiple reports of wind damage were associated with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees on Airport Road, Monroe Road and Lebanon Road in Monroe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifting north through western Maine was the focus for showers and thunderstorms during the evening of August 17th. A few of these storms produced wind damage in south central Maine during the late evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees and wires on Leighton Road in Augusta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifting north through western Maine was the focus for showers and thunderstorms during the evening of August 17th. A few of these storms produced wind damage in south central Maine during the late evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was draped across central New Hampshire into the foothills of western Maine on the morning of August 3rd. South of this boundary, a warm and very humid air mass was in place and convection began developing by late morning in the unstable warm sector. Due to the high precipitable values, a few of these storms produced wet microbursts and damaging winds. Some isolated flash flooding also occurred with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees in Jewett.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure tracking northwest of Maine drew a cold front across the region from the late evening of the 15th into the early morning hours of the 16th. Strong west to northwest winds developed in the wake of the front. Sustained winds generally ranged from 25 to 35 mph...with gusts of 50 to 60 mph...across northern portions of the region. With leaves still on many trees...the winds snapped branches and toppled trees on to power lines leading to power outages. Fallen trees also blocked some roads. In excess of 15000 customers were without power at the peak of the event during the morning of the 16th. This included customers across all of northern and eastern Maine. Power outages also occurred across central and Downeast portions of the region. However...winds were generally slightly less across central and Downeast areas. Much of the power was restored by that evening...though a small number of outages persisted into the morning of the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds generally ranged from 25 to 35 mph...with gusts of 50 to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking across the Gulf of Maine during the 24th brought precipitation to much of the region. Snow began during the early morning hours of the 24th...with warning criteria occurring during the early afternoon. The snow ended through the late afternoon into the evening. The combination of temperatures cold enough to support snow and heavier precipitation occurred in a corridor from southeast Aroostook county across northern portions of Penobscot and Piscataquis counties. Snow accumulations in this corridor generally ranged from 3 to 10 inches...with the greater totals often occurring across higher terrain areas. Lesser snow totals occurred across extreme northern Maine. South of this corridor the snow mixed with...or transitioned to...rain limiting accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 5 to 8 inches across eastern portions of the region. Locally greater totals occurred at higher elevations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracking across the Gulf of Maine during the 16th brought heavy snow to the region. Snow expanded north across the region through the morning of the 16th. Snow...heavy at times...persisted across much of northern Maine through early evening then diminished late evening. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred through the afternoon into the evening. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 6 to 10 inches. A record daily snowfall for the 16th occurred at Caribou with 9.3 inches. Although the snow transitioned to a wintry mix...a daily snowfall record of 4.2 inches also occurred at Bangor on the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 7 to 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified as it moved up the east coast to near Cape Cod, Massachusetts on November 27th. This allowed for gale force northeasterly winds to generate nearshore waves of 10 to 15 feet and increased storm surge values. This coastal flood event coincided with a period of high astronomical tides.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure combined with high astronomical tides to produce coastal flooding in coastal York County Maine. Minor flooding occurred along Granite Point Road in Biddeford. Route 9 was closed due to flooding in Old Orchard Beach near Tioga Avenue. Randall Avenue was flooded in Ocean Park, causing water to inundate shuffleboard courts in the town square.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified as it moved up the east coast to near Cape Cod, Massachusetts on November 27th. This allowed for gale force northeasterly winds to generate nearshore waves of 10 to 15 feet and increased storm surge values. This coastal flood event coincided with a period of high astronomical tides.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure combined with a high astronomical tide of 11.1 feet and a storm surge of 1.35 feet to reach a 12.45 storm tide. Flood stage is 12.0 feet. The tide remained above flood stage 102 minutes with nearshore waves of 10 to 15 feet. Minor flooding occurred on local wharf side streets near Js Oyster Bar in Portland. In Scarborough, waters surrounded the Clam Bake Restaurant. Huot's Seafood Restaurant was also surrounded with about six inches of water in Saco where splash-over significantly eroded portions of North Road, leading to flooding on Riverside Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked along a stalled frontal boundary draped across southern New England and brought widespread snowfall to the area. Late on the 19th and early on the 20th very light rain and snow persisted along the stalled front. As low pressure approached on the morning of the 20th precipitation gradually changed to all snow and became steadier. Snow continued through the day on the 20th as temperatures hovered near freezing. Low pressure continued to strengthen in the Gulf of Maine and helped to prolong the snow into the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Early on the morning of the 20th a parallel mesoscale band of snow developed over coastal western Maine, just to the north of the mid level warm front. Snow persisted under this feature into the late afternoon, where a narrow swath received 6 to 8 inches of snow. As temperatures aloft cooled in the mid-afternoon, the snow to liquid ratios became very high for the last 1 to 2 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked along a stalled frontal boundary draped across southern New England and brought widespread snowfall to the area. Late on the 19th and early on the 20th very light rain and snow persisted along the stalled front. As low pressure approached on the morning of the 20th precipitation gradually changed to all snow and became steadier. Snow continued through the day on the 20th as temperatures hovered near freezing. Low pressure continued to strengthen in the Gulf of Maine and helped to prolong the snow into the evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracking across the Gulf of Maine during the 16th brought heavy snow to the region. Snow expanded north across the region through the morning of the 16th. Snow...heavy at times...persisted across much of northern Maine through early evening then diminished late evening. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred through the afternoon into the evening. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 6 to 10 inches. A record daily snowfall for the 16th occurred at Caribou with 9.3 inches. Although the snow transitioned to a wintry mix...a daily snowfall record of 4.2 inches also occurred at Bangor on the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 6 to 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure weakened while approaching from the west through the early morning hours of the 27th while a secondary low developed and tracked toward southern Maine. The secondary low then occluded and slowly crossed the region through the 27th into the 28th. Snow expanded north across the region through the morning of the 27th. Snow persisted into the early evening...then diminished late evening. Warning criteria snow accumulations across east-central areas occurred through the late morning into early afternoon. Across northern areas...warning criteria snow accumulations occurred through the late afternoon into the evening. Storm total snow accumulations across much of east-central and northern Maine ranged from 6 to 10 inches...with local totals up to around 12 inches. However...lesser accumulations occurred across eastern portions of Aroostook county.|Strong onshore winds...large waves and a high astronomical tide contributed to coastal flooding along portions of the Downeast coast around the time of high tide during the early afternoon of the 27th. Rocks of varying size and other debris were washed onto roads at several locations including Hull Cove...Seal Harbor...Schoodic Point and Seawall Road. Road damage was also reported at Seawall Road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 7 to 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This winter storm began as a blizzard over the Midwest. The storm moved into the eastern Great Lakes early on the 26th and snow broke out over northern New England that afternoon and evening. Secondary low pressure developed in the vicinity of Cape Cod on the evening of the 26th helping to ensure cooler air remained trapped across much of western Maine. Surface temperatures hovered within a couple degrees of freezing, but aloft temperatures were much colder. As a result snowfall was heavy and wet, and very dependent on elevation. While lower elevations mixed at times with sleet, freezing rain, and rain, the higher terrain remained predominantly snow and significant accumulations occurred. Low pressure slowed to a crawl on the 27th and snow continued into the 28th, although the bulk of the accumulation was from the evening of the 26th to the afternoon of the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Steady snow developed on the evening of the 26th. Orographic lifting of southeasterly winds created heavy snow at times in the higher elevations near the Bigelow Range. Once winds became more westerly significant snow accumulation occurred north towards the Canadian border. Storm total snowfall ranged from around 6 inches in the lower elevations, to a foot or more in the higher terrain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure lifting across Maine drew a strong cold front across the region during the afternoon and evening of the 3rd. West to northwest winds rapidly increased in the wake of the cold front and persisted into the early morning hours of the 4th. Winds were sustained at 25 to 35 mph...with gusts of 50 to 60 mph. The winds toppled and snapped numerous trees along with breaking branches. The falling trees and branches brought down power lines contributing to power outages...along with snapped utility poles. Power outages rapidly increased through the evening of the 3rd into the early morning hours of the 4th. Power outages peaked at around 45,000 during the early morning hours of the 4th...which included additional power outages across central and Downeast portions of the region. Trees and utility poles blocked some roads slowing the recovery process. Winds then diminished later during the morning of the 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph...with gusts of 50 to 60 mph...toppled or snapped numerous trees and broke branches which brought down power lines contributing to power outages. Utility poles were also snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly developing low pressure formed off the Mid Atlantic coast and tracked through southern New England. Forcing resulted from an open wave aloft, which limited intensity and duration of precipitation. Despite a track near the coast, a seasonably cold antecedent air mass allowed precipitation to begin as snow before mixing with and changing to rain at the coast into the foothills. Precipitation stayed mainly snow in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow overspread the western Maine mountains early on the morning of the 13th. Snow gradually increased in intensity, with moderate snow developing in the mid-morning hours. In the early afternoon temperatures began to warm and snow mixed with rain at times, especially at lower elevations. During the mid-afternoon steady precipitation became showery before coming to an end.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Coastal low pressure developing off the Mid Atlantic spread snow across the Northeast on the evening of November 15th. While the strongest lift remained to the south, moderate to heavy snow did spread into parts of the area, especially far southwestern Maine. This was due mainly to strong warm air advection, as a strong jet streak rounded the base of the upper level low over the Ohio River Valley. This jet streak lifting northeastward over the region allowed the dry slot to move into the area after midnight, bringing steady snowfall to an end. On November 16th the coastal low pressure became dominant, and steady snow developed to its northwest across the western Maine mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around midnight a band of heavy snow lifted out of southern New Hampshire into parts of southwestern Maine. Snowfall quickly increased in intensity, and moderate snow fell for several hours during the overnight. Early morning of the 16th a dry slot moved overhead and precipitation intensity dropped off and at times mixed with sleet or rain. As low pressure strengthened in the Gulf of Maine one final band of snow developed and quickly moved northeast across the area, leaving behind light accumulations. Storm total accumulations were generally in the 5 to 7 inch range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed in a very hot and humid air mass that was in place over the region on the afternoon of July 26th. Precipitable water values around 2 inches resulted in very heavy rainfall rates with these storms. Flash flooding was reported in Lewiston and Auburn where 2 to 4 inches of rain fell in less than 3 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms dropped 2 to 4 inches of rain in less than 3 hours in Lewiston causing flooding on Ash and Bates Streets, flooding the Greyhound bus terminal where water reached a depth of 3 feet. Route 126 at Stanley Street was also flooded.  In Auburn, 6 to 18 inches of flowing water was reported on multiple streets in the vicinity of Court Street, Union Street and Minot Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and some thunderstorms developed ahead of an approaching cold front on the afternoon of July 10th. Numerous cells became severe as they moved into higher dew point air over southern Maine with damaging winds reported in several towns.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees on Route 220 in Thorndike.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed large tree limbs at the intersection of River Road and Mallison Falls Road in Newhall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees at 16 and 22 Hope Drive in Gorham.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and some thunderstorms developed ahead of an approaching cold front on the afternoon of July 10th. Numerous cells became severe as they moved into higher dew point air over southern Maine with damaging winds reported in several towns.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees and wires on Glecker Road in the Back Cove area of Portland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak, slow-moving cold front was the focus for afternoon showers and thunderstorms in a very warm and humid air mass. Numerous storms reached severe limits producing reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees on Frye Island.
